description: Syscall profile baseline (macOS, Apple M4, sample tool)
method: sample (macOS process sampler, 5s sample during mcp_transport benchmark)
note: dtrace requires sudo which is not available in this environment. Used 'sample' tool instead, which samples a running process by PID without requiring root.

__psynch_cvwait.description: pthread condvar wait — tokio reactor parking/waiting for I/O events
__psynch_cvwait.implication: This is the dominant cost — the worker thread is waiting for the I/O thread to signal readiness

__psynch_cvsignal.description: pthread condvar signal — waking the I/O thread after QuicSendStream::write_all
__psynch_cvsignal.implication: Cross-thread signaling overhead — each write triggers a signal to the I/O thread

kevent.description: kqueue event — tokio I/O polling (registering/waking for socket I/O)
kevent.implication: kqueue is the macOS equivalent of epoll — used for async I/O readiness

__psynch_mutexdrop.description: pthread mutex unlock — releasing tokio internal mutexes

quinn_proto_streams_write.implication: The actual protocol processing is very fast — overhead is in scheduling

key_findings.dominant_cost: 84% of time is spent in condvar wait (tokio reactor parking)
key_findings.cross_thread_signaling: Each write triggers pthread_cond_signal to wake the I/O thread — this is the multi-thread runtime overhead
key_findings.actual_io_is_fast: The QUIC protocol processing and actual sendto/recvfrom syscalls are too fast to appear in the sample
key_findings.kqueue_overhead: kevent (kqueue) appears in 1% of samples — I/O event registration
key_findings.implication_for_L5: The 84% condvar wait suggests current_thread runtime would eliminate cross-thread signaling, potentially saving 5-10µs per round-trip
